experiences.intentions:
Player 3: No role-specific hidden info as a Servant. On round 1 with no quest history, [1,2] is a standard 2-person opener and rejecting gives little value. Approving preserves flexibility and doesn't reveal much beyond normal baseline trust.
Player 2: No concrete role information yet from quest history or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1, approving an initial 2-person team that includes me is standard to gather information and avoid unnecessary early rejection. My vote reveals little beyond reasonable good play and does not significantly aid evil.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2] includes confirmed evil player 1, so as Good I should reject to prevent an evil-controlled quest team from passing/failing strategically. Rejecting also signals suspicion on 1 without revealing too much beyond normal good play.
Player 1: I know Player 4 is evil with me; Players 0,2,3 are unknown. The phase is team voting on my own proposed team [1,2]. As evil, having myself on the 2-person team gives us a chance to fail quest 1 immediately, which is strong. Approving my own team is consistent and does not reveal extra information beyond normal leader behavior.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate, while Player 2 is unknown/good-leaning by default. The proposed team [1,2] includes one evil and excludes me, so as evil this is a strong team to approve because teammate 1 can choose to fail or pass strategically. On round 1, approving a team with one known evil maximizes our influence while revealing little; rejecting would unnecessarily oppose a favorable setup and may make me look disconnected from 1 later.

experiences.intentions:
Player 2: I am a Good Servant on the first quest and currently on the approved team [1,2]. There is very limited role information so far: only Player 0 rejected while everyone else approved, which is weak evidence at best and not enough to infer alignments. As Good, the optimal play on quest is always to support mission success by voting pass. Choosing pass reveals nothing harmful beyond normal Good behavior and helps my team avoid an unnecessary failed first quest.
Player 1: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 0,2,3 are good-aligned or Merlin/Percival. On quest 1 with a 2-person team [1,2], as evil on the team I can choose to fail immediately. Early sabotage is usually strong in 5-player Avalon because it puts evil up 1-0 and there is no reason to preserve deep cover as much when I was the proposer and the team already passed 4-1. Failing reveals that either I or 2 is evil, which creates some suspicion, but securing the point is worth it for evil.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] excludes both known Evil players, so it is the strongest available 3-person team from my perspective. Quest 1 failed on [1,2], which strongly suggests either 1 or 2 is Evil; since I know 1 is Evil, 2 could still be Good. Approving this team maximizes chance of success while avoiding revealing too much beyond a reasonable good-player vote pattern.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team was [1,2] and had exactly 1 fail, which could be me or could frame 2; from my perspective 2 may be good. Current proposed team [0,2,3] excludes both known evil players 1 and 4, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and would succeed, giving Good momentum and information. As evil in the team-voting phase, optimal play is to reject strong good-looking teams, especially ones excluding all known evil. My reject also subtly suggests suspicion of this clean team, which helps evil more than approving does.
Player 2: Quest 1 team was [1,2] and it failed with exactly 1 fail. Since I am Good, player 1 must be Evil. Player 2 is confirmed Good from my own role. For the third slot, 0 or 3 could be the second Evil; we don't know yet. As a Good player in team voting, I should approve teams that definitely include one known Good and exclude the known Evil 1. This proposed team [0,2,3] has me plus two unknowns and avoids the confirmed Evil, which is the best available exploratory team and can generate useful info. Rejecting would risk worse teams later. My approval reveals confidence that excluding 1 is correct, which helps Good coordinate.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 team was [1,2] and it failed with 1 fail, so from my perspective that makes 2 somewhat more likely good, since 1 could have supplied the fail alone. Proposed team [0,2,3] excludes both evils, which is bad for evil if approved because good can get a clean pass and build trust. In team voting as evil, optimal play is usually to reject teams with no evil members. My vote reveals limited information; rejecting a no-evil team is standard for evil and also plausible for suspicious good players after an early fail.
Player 3: Quest 1 team was [1,2] and it produced exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 1 or 2 is evil. Current proposed team [0,2,3] keeps 2 and includes me; as a known Good player, if 2 is evil this team is risky, but if 1 was the culprit then this team could pass. As a Servant on vote phase, optimal play is to reject teams that include a prime suspect when better info is unavailable, especially after an immediate fail. My vote reveals skepticism of player 2 based on concrete evidence, which helps Good coordinate.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am evil Minion with teammate 4. Proposed team [1,4] contains both evil players on a 2-person quest, which guarantees we can fail and win immediately by reaching 3 evil quest wins. Publicly, prior fails came from teams [1,2] and [0,3,4], implicating me and 4 somewhat, but since this team can end the game for evil, optimal play is to approve despite the suspicion it may create. Approving reveals I support a risky team, but immediate win potential outweighs any informational downside.
Player 2: Two failed 2-player quests each had exactly one fail. First team [1,2] failed, and I know I am Good, so Player 1 must be Evil. Second team [0,3,4] failed with one fail, so exactly one of {0,3,4} is Evil. Since there are only 2 Evil in a 5-player game, that means players 0,3,4 contain exactly one Evil and two Good. Proposed team [1,4] includes confirmed Evil player 1, so as Good I should reject. Rejecting also reveals consistent Good reasoning and avoids sending a known Evil on quest.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [1,4], so approving guarantees an evil-controlled quest and likely another fail. As good, I should reject. This also minimally reveals information since rejecting a blatantly bad team is standard.
Player 3: Two failed 2-person quests mean each failed team likely had exactly one evil: [1,2] contains at least one evil, and [0,3,4] contains at least one evil. Since I am 3 and good, one of {0,4} is evil. The overlap-free structure suggests likely evils are 1 and 4 or 2 and 4; in any case player 4 is quite suspicious, and player 1 is also implicated by the first fail. Proposed team [1,4] could easily be double evil, and at minimum includes highly suspicious players. As good in team voting, I should reject teams that are likely to fail, especially with evil already at 2 quest wins. Rejecting also signals skepticism of the most implicated pair and helps avoid handing evil the game.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [1,4] contains both evil players. Since quests 1 and 2 already failed once each, approving this team gives evil an immediate chance to win quest 3 and likely the game by failing it. Voting approve is optimal for evil. My vote also aligns naturally with supporting my own proposed team; while it may slightly reveal confidence, the upside of getting a double-evil team through is far greater.

Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,2] excludes known evil players, so from my information it is the best available 2-person team. We are down 0-2, so rejecting a clean team only risks worse future teams and gives evil more room to manipulate. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a non-evil lineup, which is acceptable.
Player 1: I am Evil Minion with teammate 4. Two quests have already failed, so Evil only needs one more fail to win. Proposed team is [0,2] and excludes both evil players, so if approved the quest would likely pass, which is bad for Evil. Voting reject is optimal to try to force a different team and preserve suspicion patterns; my vote itself reveals little because this team is plausible to reject after mixed prior outcomes.
Player 2: Two failed 2-person quests: [0,2] failed once and [0,3,4] failed once. Since I am Good, the fail on [0,2] means player 0 is certainly Evil. The fail on [0,3,4] with 0 already Evil does not clear 3 or 4, but at least one Evil is among {0,3,4}; likely just 0, though a second Evil could be 3 or 4. Proposed team [0,2] definitely includes known Evil 0, so as Good in team voting I should reject. Rejecting also signals correct deduction to other Good players and avoids sending a guaranteed saboteur.
Player 3: Two failed 2-person quests means each failed team had exactly one evil. Teams [0,2] and [0,3,4] failed, so either 0 is evil, or 2 is evil and one of {3,4} is evil. Since I know I am good, second quest implies either 0 or 4 is evil. Combining gives strongest suspicion that 0 is evil; if 0 were good then both 2 and 4 would have to be evil. Proposed team [0,2] contains at least one likely evil and possibly both, so as Good I should reject. My reject signals consistent deduction and avoids sending a highly suspicious team on a must-win quest for Good.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail; quest 2 [0,3,4] also failed with exactly one fail, and since I was on quest 2 I know I caused that fail. That makes 0 and 3 somewhat more likely good from my perspective, while 2 is ambiguous but was on a failed quest without me. Current phase is team voting on [0,2] for a 2-person mission. As evil up 2-0, optimal play is usually to reject teams that exclude both evils, especially a small team likely to pass if both are good. Approving could accidentally let good regain ground and also gives less control. My vote reveals little because mixed voting has happened before; rejecting is consistent with many alignments and helps evil avoid a clean good success.

Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team is exactly [1,4], so it is guaranteed bad and would hand evil the third quest if approved. In team voting, optimal good play is to reject any team containing known evil. My reject may signal suspicion, but at this point preventing an auto-loss matters more than concealment.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, and this proposed team [1,4] is double evil. With evil already up 2-0, approving a team guaranteed to have evil control is excellent. Even if others suspect it, my individual approve vote is consistent with supporting my own team proposal and does not meaningfully hurt evil; if the team passes, we can likely secure the third fail and win immediately.
Player 3: I am Good Servant with limited info. Q1 failed on team [0,3,4], and since I am good, at least one of 0 or 4 is evil. Current proposed team [1,4] includes 4, who is strongly suspicious. With evil already at 2 quest fails, approving a team containing a likely evil player is too risky. Rejecting also aligns with prior voting patterns where [1,4] was already rejected.
Player 2: Two quests have failed. The approved team [0,3,4] produced exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/3/4 is evil. Repeated proposals [1,4] have been unpopular and give little reason to trust both. As a good Servant, with evil already at 2 points, I should be highly conservative and reject teams lacking me and based on weak evidence. Rejecting also pressures for a more informative/safer team next round; approving risks immediate loss if this team contains evil.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is evil with me. Quest 1 and 2 both failed, and the last approved team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, which likely increased suspicion on me or one of 0/3. Now the proposed team [1,4] contains both evil players and can secure another fail to win immediately. In team voting as evil, optimal play is to approve any team with enough evil presence to fail the quest, especially one with both evils. Approving reveals little beyond consistency with wanting this team through, and winning quickly outweighs any suspicion.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2] includes confirmed evil player 1, so it should be rejected. On team voting, optimal good play is to reject any team containing known evil. My vote reveals little beyond opposing a suspicious team, and helps avoid an evil-controlled quest.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate; team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, which is compatible with 4 failing and makes 0/3 somewhat better-looking. Current proposed team [1,2] includes me, so if approved I can fail and win immediately for evil by reaching 3 failed quests. As evil, optimal play is to get this team through. Approving helps maximize chance of quest happening; rejecting risks a different team without me. My vote itself reveals little since I can justify approving my own team slot.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest 1 and 2 both failed, with quest 2 being [0,3,4], so suspicion is likely on 3/4 and maybe 0. Team [1,2] includes my teammate and excludes me, which is strong for evil because 1 can fail and I avoid further suspicion. As evil on team voting, I should support a team that contains an evil player and can secure the third fail for an immediate evil win. Approving reveals some support for 1, but winning the game now outweighs image concerns.
Player 3: Two failed 2-person quests means each failed team contained at least one evil. Quest 1 [0,3,4] had 1 fail, and since I am good, at least one of 0 or 4 is evil. Quest 2 was not run; [0,2] and [1,4] were rejected repeatedly. Now proposed [1,2] avoids both strongest suspects 0/4 from the only actual failed quest, which is somewhat better. But with two evil among five and no confirmed good, [1,2] could still easily be double evil or include one evil. As good on round 4, rejecting a dubious 2-person team is safer because auto-pass only happens after the 5th rejection, and we still have one more chance to form a better team. My vote reveals skepticism toward an unproven pair and is consistent with trying to avoid handing evil a third quest.
Player 2: I am Good Servant with no hidden info. Two quests have already failed: team [0,3,4] had exactly one evil, and team [1,4] was proposed twice and rejected both times, which is somewhat suspicious for 1 and 4. Team [0,2] was heavily rejected despite me knowing I am good, which may indicate suspicion on 0 or coordinated evil voting. Current phase is team voting on [1,2]. As good, down 0-2, I should reject teams that include a likely evil. Since 1 was pushing [1,4] and now this team includes 1, while 2 (me) is confirmed good only to myself, [1,2] is too risky. Rejecting preserves a chance at a better composition, and with round 4 we are not yet at auto-pass. My vote reveals I do not trust 1; that is acceptable and helpful for good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] excludes both known evils, so it is the best available 2-player team. Since this is round 5, a reject would auto-pass the team anyway, so rejecting gains nothing. Approving is optimal and does not reveal more than necessary.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; team [0,3] contains neither evil player, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and could pass. Evil already leads 2-0, so preventing a clean good quest is optimal. This is round 5 of 5, so if this team is rejected the mission auto-passes with this same team; therefore my individual vote likely cannot stop the team from going, but voting reject is still the correct evil-consistent signal and avoids helping legitimize a good team. Since prior [1,4] teams got only 2 approves, there may already be enough rejects to trigger auto-pass regardless. My vote reveals mild opposition to a non-evil team, which is acceptable given we are close to winning.
Player 4: I know player 1 is evil with me, and this proposed team [0,3] contains no known evil. Since evil already has 2 quest fails, our main objective is to secure the 3rd fail. This is round 5 of 5, so if I reject and enough others do too, the team auto-passes anyway; but my individual vote is public information. Approving a team of two apparent goods can help me look good while the team is likely to pass the quest, which slightly delays victory, but rejecting a clean team at this stage is suspicious and may expose me before assassination matters. Optimal deceptive play is to approve.
Player 3: Two 2-person quests have both failed, so each failed team contained at least one evil. Teams [1,4] failed and [0,3] failed, implying at least one evil in each pair. Since [1,4] was proposed twice and kept getting rejected, table likely distrusted it. Current phase is team voting on my own proposed [0,3]. As a Good servant, optimal play is to reject teams likely containing evil, but this is round 5 of 5 so a rejection auto-passes the team anyway. My vote doesn't change passage but does signal my read. Because [0,3] already failed once with exactly 1 fail, and I know I am good, player 0 is highly likely evil. Approving would falsely endorse a bad team; rejecting preserves information and aligns with good play, even though the team will still auto-pass if total rejections reach 5.
Player 2: Two quests have failed, so evil likely controls at least one member on each failed team. Recent proposed teams [1,4] were rejected twice, [0,2] rejected heavily, [1,2] rejected, and now [0,3] is up on the final vote before auto-pass. As a good Servant, on round 5 I should avoid approving a team I have little reason to trust, especially since auto-pass may force this same team if rejected—but my individual vote should still reflect best judgment. Player 0 was on a failed quest, making 0 suspicious. We have little positive evidence for 3. Approving [0,3] risks sending a likely evil 0. My vote reveals skepticism of 0 and maintains consistency with rejecting untrusted teams; that helps good more than endorsing a bad-looking pair.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,4] includes two known evil players, so as Good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids enabling an evil-controlled 3-person quest when Evil already has 2 fails and only needs one more quest win.
Player 1: I am evil Minion with teammate 4. Proposed team [1,2,4] contains both evils, so if approved we can fail quest 4 and likely win immediately at 3 fails. Voting approve is optimal. It may look suspicious if both known evils support it, but securing an evil-heavy team on quest 4 is worth it, and votes are partially hidden in aggregate history anyway.
Player 3: I know my own team [0,3] succeeded with 0 fails, so 0 and 3 look good. The game score is Good 1 - Evil 2, so approving a risky 3-person team on quest 4 could lose immediately. Proposed team [1,2,4] excludes both strongest good-leaning players and is composed entirely of uncertain players; with likely two evil among {1,2,4}, this is very dangerous. As a good servant, optimal play is to reject high-risk teams and preserve the known-success pair. Rejecting reveals I distrust the all-uncertain team, which helps good coordinate around the only proven-success composition.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Known info: team [0,3] succeeded on a 2-player quest, so 0 and 3 are both more likely good, though one evil could have passed. Earlier tiny-team proposals [0,2], [1,4], [1,2] were all rejected, giving limited info. Current proposed team [1,2,4] excludes both members of the only successful team, which is highly suspicious. With Evil already at 2 quest wins, Good should strongly prefer anchoring on successful players 0 and/or 3. Approving this team risks game loss and reveals willingness to back an untrusted lineup; rejecting is optimal and consistent with good play.
Player 4: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate. Quest history shows two fails and one success; the approved successful team was [0,3], strongly suggesting 0 and 3 are both good. That makes Player 2 likely the remaining good player, though prior rejected teams with 1 included are suspicious to others. Current phase is team voting on my own proposed team [1,2,4], which contains two evil (1 and me), so if approved we can guarantee a fail and win immediately at Evil 3. As evil, optimal play is to approve. My vote reveals little because leaders often approve their own teams, and approving a team with myself on it is standard; rejecting would be inconsistent and could hurt our immediate win chance.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are all good. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains no evil, so if approved it will almost certainly succeed, moving good to 2 successes while evil already has 2 fails. As evil on team voting, optimal play is to reject all-good teams and try to force inclusion of 1 or 4 on quest 4. My reject slightly signals I dislike this team, but that's consistent with prior voting and worth it to prevent a guaranteed good success.
Player 4: I’m evil Assassin with teammate 1. Proposed team [0,2,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely all-good and will succeed, putting Good to 2 successes and match point. As evil, I should reject strong all-good teams, especially on quest 4. The prior success on [0,3] makes 0 and 3 look good; 2 is uncertain, but this team still has no guaranteed evil presence, so approving helps Good too much. My reject gives little away because rejecting a suspiciously clean team is consistent with many alignments.
Player 2: I’m Good with limited info. The only successful quest was team [0,3], which makes 0 and 3 somewhat more trustworthy, though a 2-person success doesn’t fully clear them. Earlier rejected teams heavily involved player 1 and player 4, which is weak evidence but those combinations were unpopular. Current team [0,2,3] contains me plus the two players from the only successful quest, making it the strongest-looking 3-person team available. As Good, on quest 4 with Evil already at 2 wins, I should approve the team most likely to succeed rather than fish for more info. Approving also aligns with the best public evidence and doesn’t reveal much beyond supporting the known-success core.
Player 3: I only know for sure that team [0,3] succeeded with 0 fails, so players 0 and 3 are likely good. Earlier teams involving 1,2,4 were repeatedly rejected and we have two failed quests total, so 1/2/4 contain the evil players with high probability; player 2 is especially suspicious being on the proposed team without any clearing info. As a good Servant on team voting for quest 4 while evil leads 2-1, the optimal play is to reject risky teams and push for teams centered on known-good members. Approving [0,2,3] would legitimize 2 and risks handing evil the game if 2 is bad. Rejecting signals preference for safer compositions and preserves good's chances.
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains no known evil and matches the prior successful team core [0,3] plus unknown 2. On quest 4 with evil already at 2 points, approving the strongest all-good-looking team is optimal. My vote reveals little because rejecting my own clean team would be suspicious and harmful to Good.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are Evil. The proposed team [0,1,3] includes known Evil player 1, so this team is very likely to fail and approving it helps Evil reach a third failed quest. In team voting, my optimal play is to reject any team containing a known Evil when a safer alternative exists, especially at 1-2 with quest 4 critical. Rejecting also avoids signaling too directly beyond normal good play, since any rational good player should oppose a suspicious team here.
Player 4: I know player 1 is my evil teammate. Team [0,1,3] includes exactly one evil (1), so if approved it can fail quest 4 and likely secure evil's third point. As evil in team voting, I should support teams containing an evil player and excluding suspicious alternatives that might lock us out. My approve vote is consistent with maximizing evil win chances; it reveals little because players often differ on reads, and getting this team through helps my side immediately.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate; 0,2,3 are uncertain, but the only observed success was on [0,3], making those two somewhat more trusted by good. Current phase is team voting on my proposed team [0,1,3]. As evil and already up 2-1, the optimal play is to approve a team containing me so I can potentially fail quest 4 and win immediately for evil. Approving is also consistent with being the proposer and does not reveal extra information beyond expected self-interest.
Player 3: Two failed quests mean likely both evils have been implicated in earlier bad teams. The only successful quest was [0,3], which strongly suggests 0 and 3 are good. Proposed team [0,1,3] keeps the known-good core of 0 and 3 and swaps in 1 instead of suspicious 2/4, who were together on a failed 3-person proposal attempt and 2 was on the earlier failed 2-person quest with 1. As a good servant, approving a team with the strongest available evidence is optimal. My vote reveals trust in the successful [0,3] pair and preference to test 1 over 2/4.
Player 2: I am Good (Servant). The only successful quest was team [0,3], which makes 0 and 3 the strongest good candidates. Teams involving 1/2/4 have not produced any success, and quest history [Fail, Fail, Success] suggests at least one evil is likely among 1 and 4, while excluding 2 from this proposal is bad because I know I am good. Current phase is team voting on quest 4 with Evil leading 2-1, so Good should only approve teams with the clearest good core. Proposed team [0,1,3] keeps the good-looking pair 0 and 3 but adds 1 instead of confirmed-good me, increasing risk. Rejecting preserves information and avoids a likely dangerous team; a reject here does not help Evil more than approving a potentially losing team.
